qa_pairs.question: How can I instruct Fluent to interpret my User-Defined Function (UDF) at runtime?
qa_pairs.answer: To have Fluent interpret your UDF at runtime, you need to add a command line into your journal file before the case/data files are read or initialized. An example command is `define/user-defined/interpreted-functions "sampleudf.c" "cpp" 10000 no`. Ensure the UDF source file is in the same directory as the journal file, and remove any managed definitions in the Fluent GUI before saving your case file.

qa_pairs.question: Does the command for interpreting a UDF change if the simulation is run in serial or parallel mode?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the command for interpreting a UDF remains the same whether the simulation is being run in serial or parallel mode.

qa_pairs.question: What is required to use an interpreted UDF with parallel jobs?
qa_pairs.answer: To use an interpreted UDF with parallel jobs, it will need to be parallelized as described in the dedicated 'Parallel' section of the documentation.

qa_pairs.question: What is the process for using a compiled User-Defined Function (UDF) on an Alliance cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To use a compiled UDF, it must first be compiled on an Alliance cluster. This process creates a `libudf` subdirectory containing the `libudf.so` shared library. Once compiled on an Alliance cluster, the `libudf` directory can be transferred to other Alliance clusters, provided the same `StdEnv` environment module version is loaded. You then uncomment the 'load' UDF line in your journal file, or use the Fluent GUI to build the UDF.

qa_pairs.question: Why can't I just copy the `libudf` directory from my laptop to an Alliance cluster for compiled UDFs?
qa_pairs.answer: The `libudf` directory cannot simply be copied from a remote system, like your laptop, to the Alliance. This is because the shared library's dependencies will not be satisfied on the cluster, which would cause Fluent to crash on startup.

qa_pairs.question: Which lines should be uncommented in my journal file for a compiled UDF?
qa_pairs.answer: You should uncomment only the 'load' libudf line in your journal file when submitting jobs to the cluster. Both the 'compile' and 'load' libudf lines should not be left uncommented, as this will lead to inefficient recompilation for every job and potential build conflicts.

qa_pairs.question: What are the consequences of leaving both compile and load UDF commands uncommented in a journal file for compiled UDFs?
qa_pairs.answer: Leaving both compile and load UDF commands uncommented in your journal file for compiled UDFs is highly inefficient because your UDF will be automatically recompiled for each job. It can also lead to build conflicts if multiple jobs are run from the same directory simultaneously.

qa_pairs.question: How can I compile a UDF using the Fluent GUI instead of configuring the journal file?
qa_pairs.answer: To compile a UDF using the Fluent GUI, run the GUI on any cluster compute node or gra-vdi. Navigate to the 'Compiled UDFs Dialog Box', add your UDF source file, and then click 'Build'.

qa_pairs.question: What is necessary for a User-Defined Function (UDF) to work with a Fluent parallel job?
qa_pairs.answer: Before a UDF can be used with a Fluent parallel job (whether single node SMP or multinode MPI), it must be parallelized. This involves adding compiler directives, predicates, and reduction macros into your serial UDF code.

qa_pairs.question: What are the potential consequences if I don't parallelize my UDF for use with Fluent parallel jobs?
qa_pairs.answer: Failure to parallelize your UDF for Fluent parallel jobs will, at best, result in Fluent running slowly, or at worst, cause it to crash immediately.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find detailed information on how to parallelize a UDF for Fluent?
qa_pairs.answer: Detailed information on how to parallelize a UDF can be found under 'Part I: Chapter 7: Parallel Considerations' of the Ansys 2024 'Fluent Customization Manual'.

qa_pairs.question: How can User-Defined Functions (UDFs) be used to customize Discrete Phase Models (DPM) in Fluent?
qa_pairs.answer: UDFs can be used to customize Discrete Phase Models (DPM) by defining particle injections through 'Point Properties' variables like source position, initial trajectory, and mass flow rate. This can be done in the Fluent GUI or automated via journal file commands.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find documentation specific to DPM UDFs in Fluent?
qa_pairs.answer: Documentation for DPM UDFs can be found in 'Part III: Solution Mode | Chapter 24: Modeling Discrete Phase | 24.2 Steps for Using the Discrete Phase Models| 24.2.6 User-Defined Functions' of the '2024R2 Fluent Users Guide' and 'Part I: Creating and Using User Defined Functions | Chapter 2: DEFINE Macros | 2.5 Discrete Phase Model (DPM) DEFINE Macros' of the '2024R2 Fluent Customization Manual'.

qa_pairs.question: How do I define particle injection 'Point Properties' for a DPM simulation in the Fluent GUI?
qa_pairs.answer: In the Fluent GUI, click the 'Physics' panel, then 'Discrete Phase' to open the 'Discrete Phase Model' box. Click the 'Injections' button, then 'Create' in the 'Injections' dialog box. In the 'Set Injection Properties' dialog, select an 'Injection Type' (e.g., 'single', 'group', 'surface', 'flat-fan-atomizer') and then use the 'Point Properties' tab to input the corresponding values.

qa_pairs.question: Can I use a text file to specify injection 'Point Properties' for DPM in Fluent?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, you can use a text file. From the 'Injection Type' pulldown in the 'Set Injection Properties' dialog, select 'file', specify the 'Injection Name', and then click the 'File' button (next to 'OK') to select either an Injection Sample File (.dpm extension) or a manually created injection text file.

qa_pairs.question: What journal file commands can automate the setup of DPM injections and continuous phase interaction?
qa_pairs.answer:
After solution initialization, you can add commands to your journal file such as:
```
/define/models/dpm/interaction/coupled-calculations yes
/define/models/dpm/injections/delete-injection injection-0:1
/define/models/dpm/injections/create injection-0:1 no yes file no zinjection01.inj no no no no
/define/models/dpm/injections/list-particles injection-0:1
/define/models/dpm/injections/list-injection-properties injection-0:1
```
This enables updating DPM source terms every flow iteration and defines injections from a specified file.
